coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent system based on graphene quantum dots-capped magnetic hydroxyapatite-MIL-100 metal-organic frameworks for pH-sensitive and controlled release of DOX
coredata.dc:creator: Karimi, Soheyla
Zeyni, Vahid
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Since metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have shown great potential as emerging candidates in biomedical applications, it is worthwhile to construct an MOF-based drug delivery system. Hence, in this work, magnetic hydroxyapatite-MIL-100 metal-organic frameworks (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100) with antioxidant properties were synthesized as a novel drug delivery system through in-situ self-assembly of MIL-100 MOFs around pre-synthesized magnetic hydroxyapatite nanoparticles. Then, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites were capped with fluorescent graphene quantum dots (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs) for loading and delivering of doxorubicin (DOX). The SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs encapsulation efficiency was 80.2, 42.2, and 95.8 %, respectively. The in vitro DOX release amount after 72 h from SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs at pH 5 was 75.8 %, 86.2 %, and 67.2 %, while at pH 7.4 was 37.3 %, 50.1 %, and 29 %, respectively. These results confirmed a pH-responsive controlled release of DOX from nanocomposites. The release mechanism of DOX from the prepared nanocomposites was also well fitted to the Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic, Fickian diffusion, and diffusion-controlled release. In addition, the MTT assays of nanocomposites against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines clearly illustrated that the prepared nanocomposites had no significant cytotoxicity, while DOX-loaded nanocomposites had notable toxicity to MCF-7 cells. The IC50 values of DOX, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-DOX, GQDs-DOX, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs-DOX against MCF-7 cells calculated at about 4.2 μg/mL, 1.8 μg/mL, 3 μg/mL, and 1.2 μg/mL, respectively. The DPPH test results also showed that the nanocomposites have excellent antioxidant activity. In addition, the in vitro hemolysis results displayed neglectable hemolysis activity (lower than 5 %) of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs even at a high dose. Therefore, these new drug delivery systems can be used safely as potential drug carriers in targeted cancer therapy.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent system based on graphene quantum dots-capped magnetic hydroxyapatite-MIL-100 metal-organic frameworks for pH-sensitive and controlled release of DOX

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Biomedicine Institute, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have shown great potential as emerging candidates in biomedical applications, it is worthwhile to construct an MOF-based drug delivery system. Hence, in this work, magnetic hydroxyapatite-MIL-100 metal-organic frameworks (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100) with antioxidant properties were synthesized as a novel drug delivery system through in-situ self-assembly of MIL-100 MOFs around pre-synthesized magnetic hydroxyapatite nanoparticles. Then, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites were capped with fluorescent graphene quantum dots (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs) for loading and delivering of doxorubicin (DOX). The SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs encapsulation efficiency was 80.2, 42.2, and 95.8 %, respectively. The in vitro DOX release amount after 72 h from SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs at pH 5 was 75.8 %, 86.2 %, and 67.2 %, while at pH 7.4 was 37.3 %, 50.1 %, and 29 %, respectively. These results confirmed a pH-responsive controlled release of DOX from nanocomposites. The release mechanism of DOX from the prepared nanocomposites was also well fitted to the Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic, Fickian diffusion, and diffusion-controlled release. In addition, the MTT assays of nanocomposites against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines clearly illustrated that the prepared nanocomposites had no significant cytotoxicity, while DOX-loaded nanocomposites had notable toxicity to MCF-7 cells. The IC50 values of DOX, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-DOX, GQDs-DOX, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs-DOX against MCF-7 cells calculated at about 4.2 μg/mL, 1.8 μg/mL, 3 μg/mL, and 1.2 μg/mL, respectively. The DPPH test results also showed that the nanocomposites have excellent antioxidant activity. In addition, the in vitro hemolysis results displayed neglectable hemolysis activity (lower than 5 %) of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs even at a high dose. Therefore, these new drug delivery systems can be used safely as potential drug carriers in targeted cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, many efforts have been made to design and synthesize efficient drug carriers to enhance drug delivery efficiency and reduce side effects [1,2]. In this regard, various nanocarriers such as micelles, polymers, zeolites, magnetic nanoparticles, hydrogels, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), dendrimers, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and mesoporous silica have been investigated for cancer treatment [3–6]. Among these, magnetic nanostructured materials, as well as MOFs have gained significant attention as promising nanocarriers for a range of biomedical applications [7–10]. MOFs are a type of crystalline porous framework constructed from inorganic metal ions and coordinated organic ligands [11]. For example, MIL-100 (Fe) MOFs contain Fe (III) and normal carboxylate-type ligands. For the past decade, MOFs have attracted great interest as an effective drug system due to their several potential advantages over other drug delivery systems such as high surface area, porous crystalline structures, non-toxic, biodegradability, large drug loading capacity, feasibility of functionalization, controlled release of drug, and high chemical and thermal stabilities [12–14]. In recent years, magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles as an ideal nanocarrier for drug delivery have received tremendous attention due to their thermal stability, non-cytotoxic, biocompatibility, large surface areas, superparamagnetic, and targeted drug delivery of drug [15–17]. Despite their unique properties, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles can undergo agglomeration which can surface coated by different materials [18,19]. Recently, hydroxyapatite (HA)-coated nanocomposites have attracted attention as efficient drug nanocarriers. These core-shell nanocomposites can enhance the biocompatibility, while also preventing premature drug release to obtain pH-controlled release of the encapsulated drugs [20–22]. Overall, by combining the excellent properties of MOFs with the magnetic property of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, nanocomposites with magnetic MOFs became ideal candidates for drug delivery due to their ability to be magnetically separated from solution after drug delivery, which can improve the efficiency and safety of the treatment [23,24]. Moreover, today, the surface functionalization of MOFs with GQDs has attracted considerable attention in biomedicine [25]. GQDs are a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with unique properties such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, high fluorescent activity, small size, excellent solubility, eco-friendliness, and high stability [26,27]. The surface functionalization with GQDs could dramatically extend the stability, biocompatibility, and strong fluorescence in the MOF nanocomposites [28,29]. Till now, different researchers have prepared MOFs as targeting drug carriers for specifically delivering drugs to cancer cells (Table 1 ). However, to date, there has been no report to use the MOFs with antioxidant properties including SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs as magnetic targeting systems for controlled delivery of the drug. Despite the advantages of MOFs as a drug delivery system, the use of MOFs has been strictly confined to their biomedical application due to a lack of selective behavior towards the targeted sites, rapid aggregation, and poor solubility in physiological media. To pass through the current limitations, designing, and synthesis of anti-aggregation MOFs that utilize porosity and high surface area an instant demand. Therefore, in this study, the preparation and characterization of a new class of MIL-100 MOFs based on magnetic hydroxyapatite (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100) were reported. Accordingly, in the present work, the coating of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 surface with GQDs can remedy these defects because of their attractive characteristics including biocompatibility, high fluorescent activity, and targeted drug delivery. Also, the integration of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 with GQDs can lead to achieving a prolonged or controlled drug delivery as well as improving bioavailability and targeting drugs to specific sites. Hence, in this study, the magnetic hydroxyapatite-MIL 100 MOFs (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100) were prepared for the first time by the in-situ self-assembly of a MIL-100 MOFs around pre-synthesized magnetic hydroxyapatite nanoparticles. Then, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 was capped with fluorescent GQDs (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs) for pH-controlled targeted release of the drug. The obtained result showed that these new drug delivery systems could be used safely for potential applications in targeted cancer therapy.

Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.9H2O), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Citric acid, Terephthalic acid, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2.4H2O), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), Ethanol, Methanol, Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 25 %), Dimethylformamide (DMF), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), Phosphorous acid (H3PO3), Disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), Calcium Nitrate Tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2.4H2O), Sodium chloride (NaCl), Sodium acetate, Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), Potassium chloride (KCl), and Acetic acid were purchased from Merck. The anticancer Doxorubicin (DOX) drug was obtained from Sobhan Co. (Iran).

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded using an IR spectrometer (Bruker instruments, model Aquinox 55) with a KBr pellet in the range 4000–400 cm−1. The XRD patterns were recorded with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 A°) using a Bruker D8 advanced diffractometer. The surface morphology and elemental composition of the designed nanocomposites were studied by LEO 1455VP field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM/EDX). The zeta potential of the synthesized nanocomposites was studied using the Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS apparatus. The pore size and specific surface area of nanocomposites were determined by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods, respectively. The magnetic properties of the nanocomposites were characterized using a Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (LBKFB VSM model) instrument at room temperature. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) measurements were performed by a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were determined by using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. A digital pH meter, PHS-3C pH meter was used for pH adjustments.

Magnetic Fe3O4 and SiO2@Fe3O4 nanoparticles were obtained by the co-precipitation method and the Stober method according to our previously reported literature [34]. In brief, 11.68 g of FeCl3.6H2O and 4.3 g of FeCl2.4H2O were dissolved in 200 mL deionized water and magnetically stirred for 30 min at 85 °C under N2 atmosphere. Then, 20 mL of NH4OH (25 %) added dropwise to the above solution until the resulting pH reaches 10. The reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min to allow the perfect spherical growth of the magnetic nanoparticles. Finally, the resultant black precipitates were separated from the solution by magnetic decantation and washed and washed with deionized water, NaCl (0.02 M), and ethanol frequently, then dried under vacuum conditions. In the following, for preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles, 5 g of prepared Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 200 mL water-ethanol solution (1:4, v/v) by ultrasonic for 30 min. Next, 5 mL of NH4OH (25 %) and 2 mL of TEOS added dropwise to the above solution, and the solution was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The dark brown products were separated by an external magnet, washed with water and ethanol several times, and dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C.

To prepare SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, the sonochemical method was used, which is an important tool in synthesis chemistry for the production of nanoparticles under ambient conditions in recent years. Due to the special properties of green capping agents such as lower toxicity, higher biodegradability, high surface activity, and environment compatibility, we used fructose as a green capping agent to prepare SiO2@Fe3O4-HA nanocomposite. Therefore, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA nanoparticles were prepared by facile precipitation (assisted by ultrasonic) route with a slight modification [35]. First, 0.07 g of the obtained SiO2@Fe3O4 powder was suspended in 30 mL of methanol for 30 min. Subsequently, a molar ratio of 1:1 (Ca precursor: C capping agents) including fructose solution (1.67 mmoL) and 0.39 g of Ca(NO3)2.4H2O (1.67 mmoL) was combined under ultrasound waves for 30 min. The resulting solution was added drop by drop to the Fe3O4@SiO2 suspension. Next, 25 % of NH4OH was added to the reaction mixture to adjust the pH 12. Finally, 10 mL of H3PO4 (1 mmoL) was added drop by drop to the resulting suspension to obtain a Ca/P molar ratio at 1.67 and stirred for 24 h at 25 °C. The obtained precipitates were collected using a magnet, washed regularly with distilled water, and dried at 80 °C.

In this work, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites were synthesized with the following process: typically, first, 0.0935 g of Fe(NO3)3.9H2O and 0.0575 g of terephthalic acid was entirely dissolved in 15 mL of DMF to give a yellow solution. Next, 0.01 g of prepared SiO2@Fe3O4-HA nanocomposite was added to the above yellow solution and completely dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min. Finally, the reaction mixture was placed in a Teflon autoclave and kept for 4 h in an oven at 185 °C. The obtained orange precipitates were removed from the reaction mixture by simply applying a magnetic field and washed regularly with DMF and methanol to remove residuals and inactive solvents. The resulting composite was dried in that vacuum for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots capped SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites (SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were synthesized using the citric acid pyrolysis method as described in our previous work with minor modifications [36]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle for 30 min. Then, the obtained orange liquid was added drop-wise to 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH at room temperature under vigorous stirring. After neutralizing with HCl (pH 7), the stock solution of GQDs was obtained. The SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites were then synthesized following procedure: first, 0.3 g of prepared GQDs were added into 20 mL of DMSO and then dispersed for 20 min. Then, the resulting solution was kept for 24 h with continuous stirring in the dark at room temperature. Also, 15 mg of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 were dispersed for 20 min in 20 mL of DMSO and then added to GQDs solution and stirred overnight in the dark at 25 °C. Finally, the obtained SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites were collected by a magnet and dialyzed (MWCO 2 K Da) against water to remove the unbound GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro studies of DOX loading and releasing
In vitro loading and releasing behavior of DOX
ce:section.ce:para:
The in vitro DOX drug loading experiments were performed using the dialysis bag method as follows: 30 mg of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites were separately added to 3 mL of DOX drug (2 ppm in PBS, pH 7.4) and sonicated for 5 min. Then, the suspension was stirred in an orbital shaker for 72 h at room temperature in the dark. In order to remove the unloaded drug, the mixture was placed in a dialysis bag and the set was placed in a beaker containing distilled water for 24 h. The beaker water was changed every 2 h. Finally, the mixture was dried by freezing and refrigerated to test drug release. The concentration of the free DOX in the supernatant was quantified by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 480 nm. The drug loading content (DLC %) and entrapment efficiency (EE %) were determined from the following formulae:
(1)

Weight of
DOX
in the nanocomposites

(2)

in the nanocomposites
mg

DOX

mg
× 100

The experiments of the DOX release from different carriers were performed using the dialysis bag method as follows: 10 mg of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-DOX, GQDs-DOX, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs-DOX respectively were dispersed for 5 min in 5 mL phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH 5, pH 6.8, and pH 7.4), and the samples were transferred into a dialysis bag. The bag was then placed in 50 mL PBS and gently shaken in the dark at 37 °C in a constant temperature water bath (Lab Tech LSB-015S) for 72 h. At different time intervals, 3 mL of samples were removed, and the volume was replaced with the same volume of fresh PBS solution. The concentration of the released DOX in the supernatant solutions was determined at 480 nm by UV–Vis spectroscopy. All the steps were carried out three times. The formula for calculating the released percentage of DOX (RD %) is:
(3)
RD % =
Amount of
DOX
released
at
a
particular time
mg

DOX

The Higuchi model and the Korsmeyer-Peppas models were applied to study the in vitro drug release data. Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models can be attributed to systems with water-soluble drugs and drug release from polymer matrices, respectively. The Higuchi model (Eq. 4) describes a Fickian diffusion behavior with the rate of drug release from polymeric matrices such as tablets and implants or other diffusion-controlled systems [37]:
(4)
Q t
=
Q 0
+
K H
t
1 / 2
where Q0 and Qt are the initial and amount of release of drug at a time “t”, respectively, and KH is the Higuchi constant. The Higuchi model is one of the most used models to describe the release of drugs from a solid dosage form or the nanoparticles with matrix structures as a square root of a time-dependent process. By using the Higuchi equation, the assumptions of its derivation must be considered which makes it rarely applicable for many controlled release systems, stimuli-responsive swellable matrices, and nanogels smaller in dimension than their payload. This model is based on the hypotheses that (i) initial drug concentration in the matrix is much higher than drug solubility; (ii) drug diffusion takes place only in one dimension (edge effect must be negligible); (iii) drug particles are much smaller than system thickness; (iv) matrix swelling and dissolution are negligible; (v) drug diffusivity is constant; and (vi) perfect sink conditions are always attained in the release environment. The Higuchi model suggests that the drug is released by diffusion. On the other hand, the Korsmeyer-Peppas model is used when the controllable process mechanism is incomprehensible, such as a combination of more than one type of release mechanism or is a combination of the diffusion of the active principle (Fickian transport) and Case II transport (non-Fickian). Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Eq. 5) describes the drug release from a polymeric system such as slabs, cylinders, and spheres with a simple relationship [38]:
(5)
Q t
=
K KP
t n
where KKP and n are the Korsmeyer-Peppas constant and release exponent describing the mechanism of release of drug form systems, respectively. In this model, n ≤ 0.45 shows the drug release is primarily controlled by Fickian diffusion; 0.45 ≤ n ≤ 0.89 represents anomalous transport, which is a combination of Fickian diffusion and other mechanisms; n ≥ 0.89 suggested that the drug release is primarily controlled by the case-II transport mechanism (non-Fickian). The Korsmeyer-Peppas model describes some release mechanisms simultaneously such as the diffusion of water into the matrix, swelling of the matrix, and dissolution of the matrix. Also, the Korsmeyer-Peppas model can help in optimizing drug delivery systems for controlled release. The main characteristics of this release model are (i) A fast increase in the concentration of solvent, with a rapid penetration to the center of the matrix; (ii) A solvent concentration that remains constant both in the swollen portion and in the swelling border; (iii) A solvent penetration border that is fast and advances with constant velocity, and a diffusion distance that is proportional to time; (iv) An induction time in the Fickian concentration profile before the penetration of solvent to the center of the matrix, which is in a vitreous state.

The cytotoxicity of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs, free DOX, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-DOX, GQDs-DOX, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs-DOX were studied via MTT assay. For this purpose, MCF-7 cells were grown in 96 well plates (7 × 103 cells per well) and incubated at 37 °C with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Afterward, the free nanocomposites and doxorubicin-loaded nanocomposites in different concentrations (2, 4, 8, 16, and 32 μg/mL) were added to the MCF-7 cell culture medium and incubated in the dark for 48 h. Subsequently, an MTT solution of 100 μL (5 mg/mL) to each well was added and incubated for another 4 h in a dark place. Finally, the formazan crystals were dissolved at room temperature by the addition of 100 μL of DMSO. The microplate reader was applied to measure the absorbance of each well at 570 nm. Three replicates were carried out for each experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antioxidant assays of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The DPPH (1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl) assay was used to measure the in vitro antioxidant activity of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites [39]. To perform the assay, a methanolic solution of 0.1 mM of DPPH was prepared and 0.3 mL of this solution was added to different concentrations of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites (5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μg/mL) and the resulting mixture was then incubated in the dark condition for 30 min at room temperature. The absorbance of the mixture was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 517 nm. The ascorbic acid and the DPPH methanol reagent without the sample were used as a standard and the control, respectively. Each test was performed for three replicates. To calculate the percent DPPH inhibition the following equation was used:
(6)
DPPH scavenging activity
%
=
A control
−
A sample
A control
× 100

To perform the hemolysis test, first, fresh cow blood was prepared from an Iranian Sarabi cow due to the similarity of the composition of cow and human blood. Then, 5 mL of cow blood was mixed with heparin anticoagulant for 5 min on a hematology mixer. After that, the prepared mixture was centrifuged for 5 min at 3000 rpm and purified by successive washing with 0.15 mM NaCl. Cow blood cell suspension was diluted ten times with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) for further use. In the next step, 0.5 mL of different concentrations of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs (2, 4, 8, 16, 32, and 64 μg/mL) were mixed with 0.5 mL of diluted cow blood cells. The mixture was then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, centrifuged at 12,000 rpm, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 541 nm. In this experiment, deionized water and PBS solution were used as positive and negative controls, respectively. Each test was performed three times. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated using the following equation:

In this work, first, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized and modified by silica to obtain SiO2@Fe3O4.

After modifying with HA, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by using Fe(NO3)3.9H2O and terephthalic acid as raw materials using a one-pot solvothermal method. Subsequently, fluorescent GQDs were attached to the surface of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 through interaction between carboxyl/hydroxyl functional groups of GQDs with Fe3+ ions. The synthesized nanocomposites were applied for the loading and delivery of DOX. The successful preparation of MIL-100 nanocomposites is confirmed by FE-SEM, Zeta potential, FT-IR, EDX, XRD, BET, UV–Vis, PL, and VSM analysis. Scheme 1 illustrates the sequential steps for the preparation of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites and DOX loading and release process.
The data obtained from VSM, EDX, UV–Vis, and PL to confirm the structural properties of the prepared nanocomposites were included in the Electronic Supplementary Material (ESI).

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to characterize the functional group of the synthesized nanocomposites. Fig. 1a shows the FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, SiO2@Fe3O4, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites. In FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the absorption bands at 584, 1631, and 3434 cm−1 are attributed to the FeO stretching vibrations, OH bending vibration, and OH stretching vibration of the absorbed water molecules, respectively [12]. In comparison with the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the SiO2@Fe3O4 nanoparticles show the adsorption bands at 1103, 950, and 794 cm−1 are assigned to the Si-O-Si stretching vibration, and the Si-OH asymmetric bending and stretching vibration, respectively, which indicates the formation of silica shell on the surface of Fe3O4 [40]. The FT-IR spectra of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA show the new bands at 1036 and 567 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of PO4 3− groups in HA. Furthermore, a small broad peak at 1446 cm−1 is due to stretching vibrations of the NH4+ group. This result confirms the successful coating of HA on the SiO2@Fe3O4 nanoparticles [35]. FT-IR spectrum of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 also exhibits an absorption band at 1681 cm−1 is assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of -COOH groups, indicating the successful coordination of Fe3+ ion with terephthalic acid. In addition, peaks at 3065, 723, and 1507 cm−1 were assigned to vibrations of CH and CC of the aromatic rings, respectively. Peaks at 2538, 2658, and 2817 cm−1 were assigned to CH aliphatic stretching vibrations. These results suggest the presence of terephthalic acid in the MOF [41]. Further, for the GQDs, the observed stretching vibration at 1725, 1567,1404, and 1084 cm−1 can be ascribed to CO in carbonyl or carboxyl groups, CC, C-OH, and CO (alkoxy) groups, respectively [42]. The attachment of GQDs towards the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 is also confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As observed, FT-IR spectra of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs exhibit a shift of absorption peaks of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA@MIL-100 due to surface modification with GQDs.

To further verify the purity and crystal structure of as-synthesized nanocomposites, XRD patterns of Fe3O4, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. As shown in Fig. 1b, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles show sharp diffraction peaks at 2ϴ, including 30.4°(220), 35.9°(311), 43.4°(400), 54.1°(422), 57.5°(511), and 63.06°(440). These diffraction peaks can be well-defined to a cubic spinel face-centered structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [43]. The diffraction pattern of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA also shows the Fe3O4 reflections as well as HA diffraction peaks [12]. These results clearly confirmed the successful synthesis of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA. In the XRD pattern of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites, the diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 25.7°(002), 28.3°(211), and 40.3°(221) correspond to the crystal faces of HA [35]. Other diffraction peaks at 11° (428), 14.2° (088), 18.2° (7911), 20.1° (4814), and 24° (6618) were also well consistent with the peaks of MIL-100 [44]. This result confirms the presence of both Fe3O4 and the MIL-100 MOFs in the nanocomposites, which confirms the successful synthesis of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100. After modification by GQDs, a wide 22.6° (002) peak in the XRD pattern of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites is related to the interlayer distance of graphene sheets, which suggests the successful incorporation of GQDs into the nanocomposites [42]. Therefore, the XRD results demonstrate that SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized.

To further investigate the porous structure of the as-synthesized nanocomposites, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms experiments were studied. Fig. 2 presents the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 2a, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA exhibits a typical IV isotherm with an H3-type hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of a mesoporous structure in a material [12]. The nitrogen isotherms of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites in Fig. 2b, c also indicated a typical type IV isotherm with a unique H4 hysteresis loop, signifying the presence of microporous structure in both MOFs. As seen, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA shows a high surface area of 30.156 m2/g, a pore volume of 0.068738 cm3/g, and a pore diameter of 9.1176 nm, while after the coating of MIL-100, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 has significantly reduced surface area of 45.9 m2/g, the pore volume of 0.116 cm3/g, and pore diameter of 10.1 nm. These data further indicated the pore-blocking effect of MOF coating. Compared to SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs also attained a decreased BET surface area (13.1 m2/g), total pore volume (0.0263 cm3/g), and pore diameter (8.04 nm). These results are probably because of the additional volume of introduced functional groups on the surface of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA. The decrease in these values indicates the successful preparation of magnetic nanocomposites.

In flowing, the zeta potential was conducted to confirm the stability of the synthesized nanocomposites as a drug delivery system at pH = 7.4, as shown in Fig. 2d. The zeta potential of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA is about +15.5 mV due to the Ca and P groups of HA [12]. For SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, the zeta potential was found to be +48 mV due to the excess of free Fe sites at the surface of the nanoparticles. Moreover, the zeta potential of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs is about −24.1 mV due to the presence of COOH groups of GQDs sheet on the surface of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs. The zeta potential data confirmed the successful synthesis of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs. The results indicated that as-synthesized nanocomposites could be used as an effective and favorable candidate for drug delivery purposes.

The morphology property of Fe3O4, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites was investigated by FE-SEM analyze. Fig. 3 shows the FE-SEM images of Fe3O4, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites. As can be seen in Fig. 3a and b, the FE-SEM images demonstrated that the Fe3O4 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA possess a spherical shape with an average diameter of ∼35 and 75 nm, respectively [12]. After the growth of MIL-100 MOFs on the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA, an octahedral morphology was observed for the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposite. From the porous size of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposite, it can also be confirmed that GQDs are suitable for encapsulation (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, it can be observed that GQDs through not only simple physical adsorption on SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 but also through chemical adsorption between iron ions and carboxyl/hydroxyl functional groups GQDs are partially combined with SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100. Meanwhile, the FE-SEM images of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs clearly showed approximately an octahedral morphology. The size and shape compatibility between SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs indicate that GQDs cannot alter the morphology of MIL-100 MOFs.

For evaluation of the anticancer drug delivery efficiency of the designed SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites, an anticancer drug, DOX, was chosen as a model drug. In this study, at first, the nanocomposites were mixed with a PBS solution of DOX at 25 °C for 72 h in the dark and finally, the loading amount of DOX in the nanocomposites was calculated at 480 nm via UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The EE % and DLC % values for SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs were 80.2, 8.02, 42.2, 4.2, and 95.8, 9.58 %, respectively. The result fully shows that the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs possessed a better drug loading and entrapment efficiency than that of both the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and the GQDs. In addition, a comparison of DOX adsorption capacity on the carriers obtained in this study with different carriers is presented in Table 2 . As the results show the synthesized nanocomposites have a higher and better absorption capacity for DOX compared to other previously reported carriers. The high drug loading content of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs might be attributed to the various interactions between DOX and the prepared nanocomposites. These interactions can be mediated by various intermolecular forces, including hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, and π-π interactions. Hydrogen bonding occurs between the NH2 of DOX and the OH of the prepared nanocomposites. Electrostatic attraction occurs between the positively charged amino group (NH3 +) in DOX and the negatively charged surface of the nanocomposites, which can facilitate the adsorption of DOX molecules onto the surface of nanocomposites. Also, the π-π interaction between the benzene ring of DOX and the benzene ring of the prepared nanocomposites can play an important role in enhancing the drug-loading capacity of the nanocomposite (Fig. 4 ). In addition, the incorporation of GQDs into the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites can further enhance the drug loading capacity by preventing the diffusion of DOX to the solution. Therefore, the designed nanocomposites can be used as a promising system for drug applications in biomedicine.

Moreover, to investigate the controlled and pH-responsive drug release behavior of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites, we employed three different pH conditions (5, 6.8, and 7.4) at 25 °C. Fig. 5 shows the release profile of DOX from the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites at 25 °C in various pH values. As shown in Fig. 5, the drug release amount for SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 is 75.8 % and 37.3 %, for GQDs is 86.2 % and 50.1 %, and for SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs is 67.2 % and 29 % at pH values 5 and 7.4 after 72 h, respectively. These results reveal that the DOX release from carriers is pH-dependent. This observation can be explained by several factors: first, at lower pH values, the protonation of the carboxylic acid groups in terephthalic acid can lead to the dissociation of Fe3+ ions from the surface of the nanocomposites, resulting in the breakage of the coordination bonds and the release of DOX. In addition, the rapid release of DOX at pH 5 can also be attributed to some extent to the dissolution of HA in the acidic solution, which results in DOX release and increases its concentration at the tumor site. This can help to improve the anticancer efficiency of the drug and minimize its damage to normal cells. However, after coating SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 with GQDs, an extended drug release time due to the slow diffusion of drugs through the GQDs shell is evident. Therefore, the prepared SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites lead to the controlled release of DOX. At acidic pH, the protonation of amine groups on DOX molecules becomes stronger, which could increase the hydrophilicity and solubility of DOX. Also, the protonation of NH2 groups in DOX (NH3+) weakened considerably the hydrogen bond between the drug and nanocomposites at low pH, which led to the more release of the drug under an acidic medium. On the other hand, at lower pH values, the hydrogen bonding between the H+ in the medium with the –OH/–NH2 groups of DOX and the –COOH/-OH groups of the prepared nanocomposites is stronger than medium with pH 7.4. These results clearly indicate that the prepared nanocomposites are excellent pH-controlled systems for the targeted delivery of drugs.

The use of mathematical models enables the evaluation of the drug release process under different conditions and can aid in understanding the mechanism of release whether it be diffusion-controlled, chemically controlled, osmotically controlled, or swelling and dissolution-controlled; models can elucidate the main determinants of the rate of this release [54]. Kinetics models accurately and reliably describe drug release systems. The release kinetics models are investigated using linear regression analysis and coefficients of correlation (R2) are used to verify the accuracy of the fitting. Until now, many models have been developed for predicting release kinetics from different drug release systems. In this study, the mechanisms and kinetics of DOX release from SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs at pH 5 were investigated using Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic models. The estimated kinetics parameters for each model are given in Table 3 and Fig. 3S. According to the obtained R2 data, the kinetics of DOX release from all the systems were well-fitted by the Korsmeyer-Peppas model. The high release of DOX at pH 5 for GQDs in compared with SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs can be attributed to the higher k values of Peppas-Korsmeyer, which indicate a higher release rate of the DOX from the systems. Moreover, the amount of n for DOX in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was found to be n ≤ 0.45, which suggests that Fickian diffusion is the controlling factor in the release of DOX. Fickian release indicates a diffusion-controlled release, in which the drugs are released from delivery by diffusion.

To investigate the biocompatibility of the synthesized nanocomposites in biomedical applications, the cytotoxicity of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites with or without DOX loading in different concentrations (2, 4, 8, 16, and 32 μg/mL) was evaluated by MTT assay using MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines. As shown in Fig. 6 , the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, GQDs, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites demonstrated no considerable toxicity on the MCF-7 cells, indicating the good biocompatibility of all designed nanocomposites. Compared with SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100, we found higher biocompatibility for the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites due to the existence of HA and GQDs on MIL-100, which could potentially enhance the biocompatibility of the prepared nanocomposites. However, after loading DOX, the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-DOX, GQDs-DOX, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs-DOX nanocomposites show high cytotoxicity than free DOX in all the concentrations. The increased cytotoxicity of DOX-loaded nanocomposites towards MCF-7 cancer cells may be illustrated by the controlled release of DOX by the carrier compared to the rapid release of free DOX into cancer cells. The IC50 values of DOX, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-DOX, GQDs-DOX, and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs-DOX in MCF-7 cells was 4.2 μg/mL, 1.8 μg/mL, 3 μg/mL, and 1.2 μg/mL, respectively. According to the MTT data, it can be concluded that our newly designed magnetic carriers are well-biocompatible and could suggest a safe system for the delivery of anticancer drugs.

In this work, the in vitro antioxidant activity of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites was measured at 517 nm using DPPH free radical method (Fig. 7 ). As known, the DPPH assay is based on electron transfer that produces a stable free radical at room temperature. This free radical is reduced in the presence of an antioxidant compound and causes a color change from violet to colorless. From Fig. 7a and c, it can be observed that with an increasing amount of the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites, the DPPH peak intensity is decreasing. This decrease in peak intensity is indicative of the ability of the nanocomposites to scavenge free radicals and reduce oxidative stress. The percentage of DPPH radical scavenging activity can be calculated based on the decrease in peak intensity of the DPPH solution in the presence of the nanocomposites. In this study, the obtained results showed that both the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites exhibited significant antioxidant activity, as demonstrated by their ability to scavenge DPPH free radicals in a concentration-dependent manner. The antioxidant activity of these nanocomposites can be attributed to the presence of HA and GQDs, which have been shown to possess antioxidant properties in previous studies. For example, Venkatachalam et al. [50] showed good antioxidant activity of the eggshell-derived hydroxyapatite compared with ascorbic acid. Also, Hu et al. [51] exhibited high antioxidant activity of GQDs for medicinal uses. The incorporation of these materials into the nanocomposites can enhance their antioxidant activity and make them suitable for use as antioxidant agents in various applications. Also, the result showed that the %DPPH radical scavenging activity of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs was higher than that of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 nanocomposites at the same concentration. The observed differences in antioxidant activity between the two nanocomposites highlight the importance of the choice and combination of materials in the design of effective antioxidant agents. The better antioxidant behavior for SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs than that of SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 can be attributed to the strong hydrogen donor behavior of GQDs. GQDs possess a large amount of OH functional groups at edge sites, which can act as strong hydrogen donors and enhance the antioxidant activity of the nanocomposites. In addition, the extensive sp2 hybridized carbon network of GQDs can facilitate free electron delocalization and stabilization, further enhancing their antioxidant properties [52]. Meanwhile, the comparison of the antioxidant activity of the nanocomposites with ascorbic acid as a reference is shown in Fig. 7d. The results showed that the antioxidant behavior of the nanocomposites was comparable to that of ascorbic acid, which is a well-known antioxidant agent. The well antioxidant behavior observed for both nanocomposites can be attributed to several factors [53–55]: (1), the presence of active oxygen species such as OH groups as strong hydrogen donors on the surface of the nanocomposites, and (2) the MOFs networks in the nanocomposites can also improve the antioxidant behavior of MOF-based nanocomposites compared to other antioxidant materials.

The in vitro hemolysis percentage as a result of incubating red blood cells of cow (RBCs) with the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 8 . As can be seen, the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites displayed neglectable hemolysis activity (lower than 5 %) in a dose-dependent manner. According to the international standard of the United States Food and Drugs Administration (USFDA), the maximum hemolysis rate is equal to 5 % for hemocompatible systems. For example, Li et al. [56] indicated that even at a high dose the hemolytic rate of the Gd(DTPA) − GQDs with excellent biocompatibility is below 5 %, which meets the established standards for biomaterials hemolysis. Therefore, the SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites with lower hemolytic of 5 % could be considered as a non-hemolytic and desirable carrier for biomedicine utilization.

In conclusion, two new drug delivery systems, SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100 and SiO2@Fe3O4-HA-MIL-100-GQDs nanocomposites with antioxidant properties were successfully designed for the first time. The successful preparation of nanocomposites was confirmed by various techniques. Meanwhile, the in vitro DOX release study demonstrated sustained and pH-sensitive release of DOX from the synthesized nanocomposites which is desirable in cancer therapy. The DOX release kinetic results also displayed that the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was the proper model to explain the DOX release mechanism from the designed nanocomposites at pH 5. The cytotoxicity studies conducted in this study showed that designed nanocomposites have non-considerable toxicity, while DOX-loaded nanocomposites had remarkable toxicity against MCF-7 cancer cells. In addition, the in vitro antioxidant behavior and hemolysis tests of the designed nanocomposites confirmed the excellent antioxidant activity and good hemocompatibility of nanocomposites as a suitable system for drug applications. Overall, the obtained results showed that the designed nanocomposites have several advantages including good drug-loading content, well antioxidant activity, good biocompatibility, fluorescent properties, magnetic separation, good blood compatibility, and targeted pH-controlled drug-release behavior. Therefore, the designed nanocomposites could potentially be used as a promising new candidate for several biomedical applications.

Soheyla Karimi and Vahid Zeyni carried out the experiment. Soheyla Karimi wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi. Hassan Namazi supervised the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.
